instructor.name: Aakash Tyagi
instructor.office_hours: Book time with Tyagi, Aakash: 101 with Aakash Tyagi (Wed, Fri)

chunks.title: Biography
chunks.content: Aakash Tyagi has a PhD in Computer Engineering in the field of Semiconductor Yield Modeling and Design for Manufacturability. He has over two decades of industry experience at Intel Corporation in end-to-end CPU development including front-end logic design, functional verification, physical design, circuit design, and silicon debug and validation. Since 2014, he has been with Texas A&M University Department of Computer Science and Engineering as a Professor of Practice where he teaches courses in Computer Organization, Systems Programming, and Software Testing and leads research and workforce development activities in hardware verification.

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Computer Organization. (3-2). Credit 4. Computer systems from programmer's perspective including simple logic design, data representation and processor architecture, programming of processors, memory, control flow, input/output, and performance measurements; hands-on lab assignments. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Additional Course Details
chunks.content: You are required to have a computing device on which you have permission to install software. Please contact the instructor if you do not have a computing device. Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) is an initiative in the college of engineering where students are required to bring their own computing device to class. The following link explains the program and provides information about approved devices:https://engineering.tamu.edu/easa/areas/academics/byod. Approved BYOD devices fulfill the requirements for this class, although many other devices are sufficient.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Perform basic logic design
• Understand the design of a simple computer system
• Understand elements that make up a CPU (Central Processing Unit)
• Design software with an understanding of how it will run on computers
• Participate in discussions that involve impact of hardware and software elements on each other and the performance of the program
• Take intermediate and advanced classes in computer architecture, systems, operating systems, cloud, parallel computing

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
The Elements of Computing Systems, Second Edition, Building a Modern Computer from First Principles
Authors: Noam Nisan, Shimon Schocken
Publisher: MIT Press
Publication Date: 2021
Edition: 2nd edition
URL for Resource: https://www.nand2tetris.org/
Notes:
We cover the first half of this book. The chapters are available free online at the above link.

This material Is: Optional
Digital Design
Authors: Frank Vahid
Publisher: Wiley
Publication Date: 2010
Edition: 2nd Edition
Notes:
We use content from this book for teaching logic design.

This material Is: Optional
Computer Systems: A Programmer's Perspective
Authors: Randal E. Bryant and David R. O'Hallaron
Publisher: Prentice Hall
Publication Date: 2015
Notes:
We refer to this book for content on memory hierarchy

chunks.title: Grade Allocation (% of total)
chunks.content:
• Graded Lab Exercises (12): 48%
• Lab Attendance: 6%
• Project (1): 16%
• Midterm Exam (1): 15%
• Final Exam (1): 15%

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Exams: Absence is marked as 0 without prior approval from the instructor. Absence requests must be made in email (tyagi@tamu.edu) at least 4 hour in advance. Absence requests without accompanying physician's note may be denied.

Project: Late Submissions will incur a flat 30% penalty. Late submissions will be allowed till 11:59pm on the last day of classes.

Graded Lab Exercises (GLE): Late Submissions will incur a flat 10% penalty. Late submissions will be allowed till 11:59pm on the Sunday of the week during which the GLE was assigned.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
TENTATIVE SCHEDULE (Subject to change during the semester)

Lab Themes (GLE* are graded lab exercises handed out on WeekX.Day1 and due by 11:59pm WeekX.Day2, so as example if labs are MW (TR) then GLE is assigned on Mon(Tue) and is due for submission by 11:59pm Wed(Thu) of Week X.

WEEK1: No LAB
WEEK2: Tool Installation + GLE1 (TBD)
WEEK3: Writing basic hdl (Inverter using NAND gate) + Writing test file + Writing cmp file + GLE2 (Basic Gates)
WEEK3: Building multi-inputs, multi-bit gates + GLE3 (multi-input, multi-bit gates, simple logic expression implementations using combinational logic)
WEEK4: MUX and Decoder + GLE4 (Modular combinational selector logic)
WEEK5: Debugging failures + GLE5 (Meaty Combinational logic implementation)
WEEK6: Basic Addition Building Blocks + GLE6 (Adders)
WEEK7: Ripple Carry Addition, Sub-busing + GLE7 (Arithmetic Logic)
==================================MIDTERM==================================
WEEK8: Clocking and BIT + GLE8 (Multi-bit Registers and Modular Memory)
WEEK9: Modular memory + GLE9 (Sequential Logic)
WEEK10: Basic assembly code + GLE10 (Basic Assembly)
WEEK11: Code with variables + GLE11 (Assembly code with variables)
WEEK12: Code with conditionals and labels + GLE12 (Assembly code with conditionals and labels)
WEEK13: BITBOT CPU (Deploy Project)
WEEK14: BITBOT Computer (Continue Project)

chunks.title: Lecture Themes
chunks.content:
WEEK1: Course Intro and Big Picture of Modern Computers, Basic Number Systems
WEEK2: Boolean Algebra, Properties and Minimization, Logic Gates and Expressions
WEEK3: Combinational Logic Design, Combinational Logic Delays
WEEK4: MUX, DMUX, Decoder, Encoder
WEEK5: Basic Addition Building Blocks + Ripple Carry Adder
WEEK6: Modular Adders
WEEK7: 2s complement and Subtractors
==================================MIDTERM==================================
WEEK8: Sequential Design, DFF, Bit, Registers
WEEK9: Modular memory
WEEK9: Assembly Language, Variables, Labels
WEEK10: Computer Architecture
WEEK11: Computer Architecture
WEEK12: Special Topics: Memory Hierarchy
WEEK13: Special Topics: Pipelining
WEEK14: Special Topics: Parallel Processing

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: You are permitted to use Al as your assistant and tutor where applicable and with caution. Do not outsource your graded work to Al.
